metadata.title: Binomial Tree Option Pricer — CRR Model with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The CRR tree converging to Black-Scholes as N climbs from 5 to 100, with u equal to exp of sigma root T over N and risk-neutral p pinning the expected return to the risk-free rate, made the discrete-to-continuous link click. I want to extend it. Help me deepen: (1) explain how switching the European call to an American put forces an early-exercise check at every interior node; (2) walk me through why that early-exercise premium appears for puts but usually not calls; (3) show me how the convergence curve to Black-Scholes changes once early exercise is allowed.

props.command: The CRR tree converging to Black-Scholes as N climbs from 5 to 100, with u equal to exp of sigma root T over N and risk-neutral p pinning the expected return to the risk-free rate, made the discrete-to-continuous link click. I want to extend it. Help me deepen: (1) explain how switching the European call to an American put forces an early-exercise check at every interior node; (2) walk me through why that early-exercise premium appears for puts but usually not calls; (3) show me how the convergence curve to Black-Scholes changes once early exercise is allowed.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

Black-Scholes is a continuous-time formula. But you can derive the same answer from a discrete model — the binomial tree. At each step, the stock can go up by factor u or down by factor d. Cox, Ross, and Rubinstein showed in 1979 that as you add more steps, the binomial tree converges to Black-Scholes. Today we build the CRR tree with Claude for N equals 5, then run the convergence study up to N equals 100.
beats.visual_intent: title card: binomial tree branching diagram, convergence to Black-Scholes

beats.narration_text: Type the prompt. Build the CRR binomial tree for N equals 5 steps: S 100, K 100, T 0.5, r 5 percent, sigma 20 percent. Compute u, d, and the risk-neutral probability p. Compute the European call by backward induction. Run N from 5 to 100 and show convergence to the Black-Scholes value.

props.command:
claude "Write binomial_tree.py: build CRR binomial tree N=5 steps:
S=100, K=100, T=0.5, r=0.05, sigma=0.2. u=exp(sigma*sqrt(T/N)),
d=1/u, p=(exp(r*T/N)-d)/(u-d). Compute European call by backward
induction. Run N=[5,10,20,50,100] and show convergence to B-S (~$10.45)."

beats.narration_text: Read it. u is exp of sigma times root of T over N — the up factor. d is one over u. The risk-neutral probability p ensures the expected return equals the risk-free rate. At each terminal node, the payoff is max of S minus K, zero. Then backward induction: each interior value is the discounted expected value under the risk-neutral measure — p times the up value plus one minus p times the down value, discounted at r.

props.code:
# binomial_tree.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np

def crr_call(S, K, T, r, sigma, N):
    dt = T / N
    u = np.exp(sigma * np.sqrt(dt))
    d = 1 / u
    p = (np.exp(r * dt) - d) / (u - d)   # risk-neutral prob
    disc = np.exp(-r * dt)
    # terminal payoffs
    ST = S * u**np.arange(N, -1, -1) * d**np.arange(0, N+1)
    V = np.maximum(ST - K, 0)
    # backward induction
    for _ in range(N):
        V = disc * (p * V[:-1] + (1-p) * V[1:])
    return V[0]

beats.narration_text: Run it. The Manim shows the N equals 4 binomial tree: nodes at each step, edges connecting parents to children. Stock prices increase up-left and decrease down-left. The terminal payoffs in crimson are the option values at expiry. The tree is symmetric — up then down gives the same node as down then up, so there are only N plus 1 terminal nodes for N steps.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: binomial tree with 4 steps, nodes as circles, stock prices inside, crimson payoff values at terminal nodes

beats.narration_text: The European call assumes no early exercise. Now change it to an American put — early exercise is possible at every node. The binomial tree handles American options trivially: at each node, take the maximum of the intrinsic value and the continuation value.

props.command:
claude "update binomial_tree.py:
  -> compute American put option (K=100, early exercise allowed)
  -> at each node: V = max(K-S, disc*(p*Vu + (1-p)*Vd))
  -> compare American put vs European put at N=100"

beats.narration_text: American put is worth more than European put when the risk-free rate is positive — the early exercise premium. The binomial tree captures this naturally; Black-Scholes cannot price American puts in closed form. That is why the tree is still used in practice for path-dependent and American options, even though Black-Scholes is faster for Europeans.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: European vs American put comparison at N=100; early exercise premium

beats.narration_text: Your move: build the trinomial tree — three branches at each node, up, flat, down. It converges faster than the binomial tree. Also try the Longstaff-Schwartz Monte Carlo method for American options — a completely different approach using regression to estimate the continuation value.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: trinomial tree diagram, Longstaff-Schwartz regression idea

beats.narration_text: Then price a barrier option — the tree handles it by zeroing out nodes that cross the barrier. The Manim for that is a binomial tree where nodes above the barrier are crossed out, showing how the barrier kills the path.
beats.visual_intent: code snippet: barrier option on binomial tree — zero payoffs above barrier

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Your move: build the trinomial tree — three branches at each node, up, flat, down. It converges faster than the binomial tree. Also try the Longstaff-Schwartz Monte Carlo method for American options — a completely different approach using regression to estimate the continuation value. Then price a barrier option — the tree handles it by zeroing out nodes that cross the barrier. The Manim for that is a binomial tree where nodes above the barrier are crossed out, showing how the barrier kills the path.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Binomial Tree Option Pricer — CRR Model with Claude] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
